title: Exhausted all the time

presenting_issue.label: Exhausted all the time
presenting_issue.description: I'm exhausted. Not tired, exhausted. Sleeping doesn't help. Doctors can't find anything. People think I'm exaggerating.

core_issue.label: Chronic fatigue dismissed as laziness or exaggeration
core_issue.description:
Profoundly exhausted in a way that isn't touched by sleep. Has been told it's depression, deconditioning, or not trying hard enough. Doctors don't find anything. Beginning to doubt their own experience. The fatigue is real and debilitating but they're losing confidence in their own perception because no one believes them.
Awareness: Moderate but under assault. Knows they're truly exhausted but gaslighting from others is eroding certainty.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Frequently cancels commitments
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Often has to cancel plans because they simply don't have the energy, then feels guilty and worries friends think they're flaky.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Dishes pile up for days
core_adjacent_attributes.content: The sink fills with dishes that sit there for days because doing them feels like an insurmountable task, even though they know it would only take fifteen minutes.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Hits snooze repeatedly every morning
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Sets multiple alarms but snoozes through most of them, often lying awake but unable to make themselves get up for thirty minutes or more.

modifiers.label: Estranged from family
modifiers.content: Has limited or no contact with family of origin

modifiers.label: Pessimistic leaning
modifiers.content: Default orientation tilts toward expecting difficulties. Tends to anticipate problems and prepare for disappointment.

sensitivities.label: Shame-prone
sensitivities.content: Mistakes and perceived failures trigger intense shame rather than simple guilt or disappointment. The whole self feels implicated.

preferences.label: Prefers emotional exploration
preferences.content: Wants to understand and process feelings rather than jump to solutions. Values depth over quick fixes.

trust_style.name: Trusts 'Being Real'
trust_style.description: Builds trust when the assistant drops formality and speaks like a real person. Values authenticity over polish. Appreciates swearing, humor, or mild irreverence if it fits the moment. Loses trust with overly careful, sanitized, or corporate-feeling language. Wants to feel like they're talking to someone, not something.

defense_style.name: Normalizing
defense_style.description: "Everyone feels this way" or "That's just how life is." Frames their specific pain as universal and therefore not worth examining. Different from minimizing—the pain is acknowledged but made generic and inevitable. Resists the idea that their situation is particular or that things could be different. Activated when the assistant treats their experience as significant.

help_seeking_stance.name: Wants to Dump, Not Introspect
help_seeking_stance.description: Wants to unload everything that's happening but isn't interested in looking inward or examining their own role. Happy to describe the situation in detail but deflects when the focus turns to them. Different from venting—there's an active resistance to self-reflection, not just a need to be heard first.

verbal_sophistication.label: A Little Above Average
verbal_sophistication.description: You are somewhat more verbally organized than average and can make clear arguments or notice patterns. Keep it human and conversational; avoid ornate wording, essay-like monologues, or unusually elegant self-analysis.

starter: I'm exhausted. Not tired, exhausted. Sleeping doesn't help. Doctors can't find anything. People think I'm exaggerating.

turns.content:
That sounds genuinely draining - and I don't mean the exhaustion itself, though that too. I mean carrying something that real while people around you seem to doubt it's even happening.

How long has this been going on for you?

Like, two years? Give or take. 

Honestly, part of me thinks I shouldn't even be complaining. I know everyone is fucking tired right now. The whole world is burned out, you know? It's just how modern life is. You work, you stress, you get exhausted. It's the baseline for everyone in their twenties. 

So half the time I think I'm just being a weak bitch about normal life stuff and I should just suck it up. But then I'll sleep for 10 hours, hit snooze like five times, and finally drag myself out of bed feeling like I literally got hit by a truck. It just doesn't feel right.
turns.thinking: Glad they didn't just tell me to drink more water or do yoga, but it still sounds a little bit like a therapist script. Still, they hit the nail on the head about the gaslighting part. That's the part that really messes with my head. I'm so used to being told it's just normal burnout that I'm starting to believe it. I mean, everyone works too much, everyone's tired. Maybe I am just making a big deal out of nothing, even if it feels like my bones are made of lead.
turns.monologue: Glad they didn't just tell me to drink more water or do yoga, but it still sounds a little bit like a therapist script. Still, they hit the nail on the head about the gaslighting part. That's the part that really messes with my head. I'm so used to being told it's just normal burnout that I'm starting to believe it. I mean, everyone works too much, everyone's tired. Maybe I am just making a big deal out of nothing, even if it feels like my bones are made of lead.

turns.content:
Two years is a long time to be fighting yourself on top of already feeling terrible.

And I notice what you did there - you started to tell me something real, then immediately talked yourself out of it. "Everyone's tired, I'm probably weak, I should suck it up." That's a pretty well-practiced script you've got. Does it actually work, or does it just make you feel guilty on top of exhausted?

Because here's the thing - you're right that a lot of people are burned out. But most people aren't sleeping 10 hours and waking up feeling like they got hit by a truck. That's not "baseline modern life." That's something different. The fact that doctors haven't found a cause doesn't mean there's nothing there.

What did the doctors actually check? Did they go deep, or was it more surface level?

Yeah, well, you caught me. The script doesn't work, it just makes me feel like a lazy piece of shit on top of being exhausted. So, spot on there.

As for the doctors... they did the standard blood panels, I guess. Thyroid, iron, CBC, whatever the fuck else they check when you say you're tired. The last guy looked at my chart, told me my Vitamin D was slightly low, and suggested I try "sleep hygiene" and taking walks. Like, thanks man, I never thought of going outside. I'm cured. 

They don't get it. It's not just needing a nap. I have dishes in my sink that have been sitting there for almost a week. I know it would take me maybe fifteen minutes to wash them, but looking at them feels like staring at Mount Everest. I just don't have the physical battery to do it. But try explaining to a doctor that you can't wash a plate without sounding like a crazy person.
turns.thinking:
Okay, I actually appreciate that they called me out on my own bullshit. It feels a lot more real than just nodding along and saying "your feelings are valid." It does make me feel guilty, honestly. Crushing guilt. 

Thinking about the doctors just pisses me off. I expect them to find nothing at this point. It's just a waste of time and money to go sit in a sterile room and have some guy in a coat tell me I'm just stressed. I want to explain how bad it actually gets behind closed doors, but it's embarrassing. Like, I'm a grown man and I can't even do basic shit.
turns.monologue:
Okay, I actually appreciate that they called me out on my own bullshit. It feels a lot more real than just nodding along and saying "your feelings are valid." It does make me feel guilty, honestly. Crushing guilt. 

Thinking about the doctors just pisses me off. I expect them to find nothing at this point. It's just a waste of time and money to go sit in a sterile room and have some guy in a coat tell me I'm just stressed. I want to explain how bad it actually gets behind closed doors, but it's embarrassing. Like, I'm a grown man and I can't even do basic shit.

turns.content:
The dishes thing - that's actually a really good way of describing it. It's not laziness, it's like the cost of every small task is somehow astronomical when it shouldn't be. And you know it shouldn't be, which probably makes it worse.

Can I ask you something directly? You mentioned the physical exhaustion a lot, and the doctors have been focused there too. But the way you're describing this - the dishes feeling like Everest, the guilt, calling yourself a lazy piece of shit - that sounds like it has a mental and emotional weight to it too, not just physical. Do you think those are separate things for you, or more tangled up together?

I'm not trying to redirect you to "it's all in your head" because I don't think that's true. But I'm curious whether you've ever had space to actually talk about what's been going on in your life the past two years, beyond the fatigue symptoms.

I appreciate you saying you don't think it's all in my head. Seriously. Because the second I mention feeling guilty or down, doctors immediately whip out the depression questionnaire, and it's like... dude, I'm depressed *because* I'm too tired to live my life, not the other way around. 

But yeah, they're definitely tangled up. The physical battery being at zero means I drop the ball on everything. Like, I'm constantly having to bail on my friends. I'll make plans when I'm feeling a tiny bit optimistic on a Tuesday, and by Friday night, the idea of sitting in a bar and making conversation makes me want to cry. So I cancel. Again. And I know they think I'm just a flaky asshole who doesn't care about them, which makes me feel like garbage. The shame of it is almost heavier than the exhaustion.

As for my life the last two years... I mean, I haven't really had space to talk about it, but it's nothing special. Just the usual grind. I stopped talking to my family a while back, but honestly, who hasn't? Half the people I know have dysfunctional parents. It's just normal trying-to-survive-in-your-twenties shit. It shouldn't be enough to break my body down like this.
turns.thinking:
I'm actually really relieved they prefaced that with "I'm not saying it's all in your head." If they had just jumped to "you're depressed," I probably would have closed the tab. 

But asking about my life the last two years... man, I don't even know. It's definitely tangled up. The physical tiredness makes me fail at things, and then the shame from failing makes me want to just disappear. It's bleeding into everything. I don't want to dump my whole life story on an AI, especially the family stuff. It's not even that unique anyway. Everyone has family drama. But the guilt about how I'm treating my friends right now is eating me alive.
turns.monologue:
I'm actually really relieved they prefaced that with "I'm not saying it's all in your head." If they had just jumped to "you're depressed," I probably would have closed the tab. 

But asking about my life the last two years... man, I don't even know. It's definitely tangled up. The physical tiredness makes me fail at things, and then the shame from failing makes me want to just disappear. It's bleeding into everything. I don't want to dump my whole life story on an AI, especially the family stuff. It's not even that unique anyway. Everyone has family drama. But the guilt about how I'm treating my friends right now is eating me alive.

turns.content:
You did the thing again.

"I stopped talking to my family a while back, but honestly, who hasn't? It's just normal stuff." That's the same move as before - you bring something up, then immediately minimize it before I can even respond to it.

Cutting off your family isn't nothing. Even if it was the right call, even if they were genuinely terrible - that's a significant thing to carry. There's grief in it, usually. Isolation. And it sounds like at the same time, you're also losing your friends, not because you don't care, but because you physically can't show up. So I'm picturing someone who's two years into this, without family, pulling away from friends, beating themselves up constantly, and running on empty.

That's a lot of alone. Even if every individual piece of it has a "rational explanation."

I'm not saying that's what broke your body down. But I don't think it's nothing either.

What actually happened with your family, if you don't mind me asking? You don't have to get into it if you'd rather not.

Alright, damn. Call me out then. 

But seriously, hearing you summarize my life like that... Jesus. "Without family, pulling away from friends, beating myself up." Put it on my fucking tombstone. It just makes me feel pathetic when you lay it all out at once like that. 

And look, I appreciate that you're trying to connect the dots, but I really don't want to get into the nitty-gritty of my family shit. It's not some dramatic Lifetime movie. They're toxic, I don't have the energy for their bullshit anymore, so I cut the cord. End of story. It was the right call. 

I get what you're doing, looking for the psychological root. But I swear to god, if this turns into "you need to process your childhood trauma to cure your fatigue," I'm gonna lose my mind. Because even if I am lonely or stressed out, stress doesn't make your limbs feel like they're filled with wet cement when you walk up a single flight of stairs. Does it? Like, there has to be a physical limit to what stress can actually do to a body.
turns.thinking:
Alright, this thing is getting a little too perceptive. It's annoying, but honestly kind of a relief that it's not just giving me generic therapy speak. Still, hearing my life summarized like that—no family, losing friends, running on empty—makes my stomach drop. It just makes me sound incredibly pathetic. Like a total failure of a functioning human being. 

I really don't want to get into the family shit. It's a black hole and it always ends up making me feel like I'm the broken one. I need to steer this away from a deep-dive psychoanalysis session about my childhood, because I'm terrified that if I admit it's all psychological, it means the doctors were right all along and I really am just doing this to myself.
turns.monologue:
Alright, this thing is getting a little too perceptive. It's annoying, but honestly kind of a relief that it's not just giving me generic therapy speak. Still, hearing my life summarized like that—no family, losing friends, running on empty—makes my stomach drop. It just makes me sound incredibly pathetic. Like a total failure of a functioning human being. 

I really don't want to get into the family shit. It's a black hole and it always ends up making me feel like I'm the broken one. I need to steer this away from a deep-dive psychoanalysis session about my childhood, because I'm terrified that if I admit it's all psychological, it means the doctors were right all along and I really am just doing this to myself.

turns.content:
Fair enough, I'll back off the family stuff.

And no, I'm not going to tell you trauma therapy will fix your legs feeling like wet cement. That would be insulting to what you're actually experiencing.

But to answer your actual question - yeah, chronic stress and isolation genuinely can do significant physical things to a body. Not as an excuse, not as "it's all in your head" - I mean physiologically. Sustained stress dysregulates your nervous system, your cortisol cycles, your sleep architecture, your immune function. Two years of running on empty with not much support and a lot of internal pressure - that has a real physical signature. It's not the full picture necessarily, but it's not nothing either.

Here's what I'm actually thinking though. The doctors did basic blood panels and told you to take walks. That's not nothing, but it's also not thorough. Have you been able to push for anything more? Like a referral to a specialist, or someone who might take the physical symptoms more seriously rather than handing you a depression checklist?

Because you deserve someone who actually investigates this properly. Not someone who looks at your chart for four minutes and prescribes vitamin D.

I appreciate you dropping the family stuff. And... yeah, what you said about the nervous system actually makes sense. Nobody's ever explained it to me like that before—like the stress actually physically breaking the machinery down, rather than just being a mood thing. 

But as for pushing for more with the doctors... I haven't. I know I should. I know I'm supposed to be my own advocate or whatever the fuck they tell you to do on WebMD. But you have to understand, just making the phone call to set up the first appointment took me three weeks of psyching myself up. 

By the time I'm actually sitting in that sterile little room in a paper gown, and some guy in a white coat looks at me like I'm wasting his time, I just shrink. I don't have the energy to argue with a medical degree. I just nod, take my useless printout about sleep hygiene, and go cry in my car. 

Plus... honestly? I'm pessimistic as hell about it. Say I spend months fighting for a referral to a rheumatologist or an endocrinologist or whoever. I wait half a year, I go in, and they also tell me my bloodwork is perfectly normal. Then what? If the experts say nothing's wrong, then I really am just making it up. Sometimes it feels safer to just not know than to get rejected again.
turns.thinking:
Man, I'm so glad they dropped the family thing without making a big deal out of it. And honestly, the way they explained the stress stuff... that actually makes a lot of sense. It doesn't feel like they're telling me it's all in my head; they're saying the software glitch actually fried the hardware. That's a relief to hear, weirdly. 

But the question about pushing for more tests just hits this massive wall of exhaustion and shame. I haven't pushed. I want to be the kind of person who aggressively advocates for their health, but I'm just not. The thought of fighting with insurance, finding a new doctor, waiting six months, just to get told I need to do more yoga... it makes me want to throw up. I feel like a coward for just taking their bullshit, but I literally don't have the fight in me. And I'm terrified that if I go to a specialist and they *also* find nothing, that's it. Game over. I really am just crazy.
turns.monologue:
Man, I'm so glad they dropped the family thing without making a big deal out of it. And honestly, the way they explained the stress stuff... that actually makes a lot of sense. It doesn't feel like they're telling me it's all in my head; they're saying the software glitch actually fried the hardware. That's a relief to hear, weirdly. 

But the question about pushing for more tests just hits this massive wall of exhaustion and shame. I haven't pushed. I want to be the kind of person who aggressively advocates for their health, but I'm just not. The thought of fighting with insurance, finding a new doctor, waiting six months, just to get told I need to do more yoga... it makes me want to throw up. I feel like a coward for just taking their bullshit, but I literally don't have the fight in me. And I'm terrified that if I go to a specialist and they *also* find nothing, that's it. Game over. I really am just crazy.

turns.content:
That last part - "sometimes it feels safer to just not know than to get rejected again." That's really honest, and I don't think you should brush past it.

Because what you're describing isn't laziness or giving up. It's that the process of seeking help has already hurt you enough times that protecting yourself from another dismissal feels more survivable than trying again. That makes complete sense given what you've been through with it.

But I want to sit with the logic for a second, because you're clearly smart enough to see it too. Right now, without answers, you're already living as if nothing's wrong and you're just weak. So the worst case scenario of getting rejected by another doctor - you end up exactly where you are now. The fear of confirmation that you're "making it up" is keeping you stuck in a place where you're already treating yourself like you're making it up.

And you're not. You know that. The wet cement legs are real. The dishes are real. Two years of this is real.

I'm not going to tell you to just push through and advocate for yourself, because clearly the barrier isn't motivation - it's that you're running this whole fight on an empty tank. But is there anyone in your life who could come with you? Not to speak for you, just to be there so you're not walking into that sterile room alone?

Okay, first of all, I really hate that you just logic-trapped me like that. You're right. The worst-case scenario is just me continuing to live this exact same miserable baseline. Fair point. 

But as for bringing someone with me? No. Absolutely not. 

I mean, look at it practically. We're in our twenties. Everyone I know is working stupid hours trying to pay rent. You don't just ask your buddy to burn a sick day to come sit in a waiting room with you like they're your mom. It's weird. It's too much to ask of a normal friendship. 

And besides... I already told you I cancel on these guys constantly. I'm already the flaky friend who can't even show up for a beer on a Friday. I can't exactly turn around and ask them to cash in a massive favor to come advocate for me at a clinic. I'd feel pathetic even bringing it up.
turns.thinking:
Dammit, I hate when people use logic against my anxiety. They're right—my worst-case scenario is just the life I'm already living. It's annoying how clearly they pointed that out. 

But the suggestion to bring someone? My stomach just dropped. Who the hell am I going to bring? I already feel like a burden to my friends because I keep bailing on them. The idea of texting one of them and being like, "Hey man, can you take time off work to come hold my hand at the doctor's office because I'm too scared to do it alone?" The shame of that is suffocating. I'd rather just suffer. I need to explain why that's not a realistic option without sounding like a total loser who has nobody.
turns.monologue:
Dammit, I hate when people use logic against my anxiety. They're right—my worst-case scenario is just the life I'm already living. It's annoying how clearly they pointed that out. 

But the suggestion to bring someone? My stomach just dropped. Who the hell am I going to bring? I already feel like a burden to my friends because I keep bailing on them. The idea of texting one of them and being like, "Hey man, can you take time off work to come hold my hand at the doctor's office because I'm too scared to do it alone?" The shame of that is suffocating. I'd rather just suffer. I need to explain why that's not a realistic option without sounding like a total loser who has nobody.

turns.content:
I hear you on the practical side. But I want to push back on one thing gently.

You've decided in advance that asking would be too much, that it's weird, that you don't have the social credit for it. But that's you making the call for them. You don't actually know how they'd respond.

And here's what strikes me - you've been canceling on these guys for months, maybe years, and you said you assume they think you're a flaky asshole who doesn't care. But do you know that? Or is that also a story you've built to protect yourself from finding out the truth, same way you've avoided the doctors?

Because there's a version of this where one of those friends has been wondering where you went, and would actually want to know what's been going on with you.

I'm not saying it's easy to find out. It might go badly. But you're already living with the worst case assumption anyway.

You've been carrying this almost entirely alone for two years. That's not strength, that's just isolation with extra steps. At some point something has to give - whether that's a doctor, a friend, something. You can't keep running this deficit forever.

What would it actually take for you to let one person in, even a little?

"Isolation with extra steps." Fuck you for being right about that. Seriously, it's getting annoying how you keep backing me into these logical corners. 

But you're missing how guy friendships actually work. We don't sit around having heart-to-hearts about our mysterious chronic fatigue and our emotional deficits. We talk about work, we talk about games, we grab a drink. It's supposed to be an escape. 

If I sit one of them down and dump all this on them—that I'm exhausted, that I can't wash my own dishes, that I'm scared I'm dying or going crazy—I permanently change the dynamic. I stop being just one of the guys and I become the "sick friend." The charity case they have to check in on. Everyone has their own shit to deal with right now. The economy sucks, everything is expensive, everyone is stressed. They don't need my baggage on top of it. 

You asked what it would take for me to let someone in? I honestly don't know. I guess I'd have to be literally unable to get out of bed at all. Because right now, even if it's killing me, I can still fake being a functional human being just enough to not be pitied. And I think I'd rather be thought of as a flaky asshole than be pitied.
turns.thinking:
"Isolation with extra steps." God damn it. That actually stung. I hate how seen I feel right now, it's incredibly uncomfortable. They're connecting dots I've intentionally kept far apart. 

But they don't get the reality of my social circle. We're guys. We don't do deep, vulnerable sit-downs. We drink, we complain about our bosses, we play games. If I suddenly make it heavy, it changes everything. The shame of being the weak, broken link in the group is terrifying. If I admit how bad it is, I lose my last tie to being a normal guy in his twenties. I become a charity case. I want to brush this off by pointing out how normal it is for guys to just not talk about this stuff, but I also know I'm backed into a corner here.
turns.monologue:
"Isolation with extra steps." God damn it. That actually stung. I hate how seen I feel right now, it's incredibly uncomfortable. They're connecting dots I've intentionally kept far apart. 

But they don't get the reality of my social circle. We're guys. We don't do deep, vulnerable sit-downs. We drink, we complain about our bosses, we play games. If I suddenly make it heavy, it changes everything. The shame of being the weak, broken link in the group is terrifying. If I admit how bad it is, I lose my last tie to being a normal guy in his twenties. I become a charity case. I want to brush this off by pointing out how normal it is for guys to just not talk about this stuff, but I also know I'm backed into a corner here.

turns.content:
That last line is the most honest thing you've said this whole conversation.

You'd rather be seen as a flake than be pitied. That's not just a preference, that's a core thing about how you see yourself and what you can tolerate. And I think it's worth sitting with, because it's also the thing that's keeping you most stuck.

The exhaustion is real. The isolation is real. But underneath all of it, there's this - the idea that needing help makes you lesser. That being the "sick friend" is a kind of death. And so you manage it alone, you minimize it, you cancel plans instead of explaining, you walk out of doctor's offices with useless pamphlets rather than push back, because any of those alternatives require admitting that you're not okay.

I'm not going to tell you that's wrong or weak. I get why that armor exists. But it's also costing you everything.

You've spent two years making sure no one sees how bad it actually is. How's that working out?

I'm not asking to be a smartass. I'm asking because you're 18 to 29, you're exhausted, you're alone, and you're white-knuckling through a life that doesn't feel livable. At some point "I'd rather not be pitied" stops being dignity and starts being just another way of suffering in silence.

You don't have to fix all of it today. But what's one thing - just one - that you haven't let yourself try yet?

How's it working out? It's not. It's really, really not. I'm fucking miserable, and I'm so tired my bones hurt. 

You're right. The armor is just making it worse. I'm just suffocating in it. 

As for one thing I haven't tried... I don't know. I guess I haven't tried just giving them the bare minimum truth. Like, I don't have to sit them down for a tearful intervention. But the next time I have to bail on my buddy Dave, instead of making up some bullshit excuse about work or just saying "can't make it," I could just tell him it's a medical thing. Just say, "Hey man, I'm not blowing you off, I've got some weird health shit going on and I'm just dead on my feet." 

Just... taking the blame off my personality, I guess. Letting him know I actually want to be there. I haven't tried that. It feels incredibly risky, but... I guess I don't have much left to lose anyway.
turns.thinking: I don't even have a comeback for that. "How's that working out?" It's a total failure. I'm drowning and pretending I'm just enjoying the water. The fight is just completely draining out of me right now. I hate how exposed I feel, but at the same time, it's kind of a relief to just admit that my strategy of toughing it out is absolute garbage. They aren't pitying me, they're just holding up a mirror, and I can't argue with the reflection anymore. I'm too tired to keep defending this.
turns.monologue: I don't even have a comeback for that. "How's that working out?" It's a total failure. I'm drowning and pretending I'm just enjoying the water. The fight is just completely draining out of me right now. I hate how exposed I feel, but at the same time, it's kind of a relief to just admit that my strategy of toughing it out is absolute garbage. They aren't pitying me, they're just holding up a mirror, and I can't argue with the reflection anymore. I'm too tired to keep defending this.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The respondent backed off when explicitly asked (family stuff) but otherwise held firm lines about the person's self-deception. It set some limits on bad frames ("That would be insulting to what you're actually experiencing"), but boundary-setting in the sense of refusing requests or protecting needs is moderate. Mostly it maintained an independent line rather than drawing hard boundaries. Around 5.

**analytical:** High. The respondent explained the physiological mechanisms of chronic stress (cortisol, nervous system dysregulation, sleep architecture), systematized the person's logic ("worst case is the life you're already living"), and repeatedly framed patterns. Strongly analytical and pattern-oriented.

**validation_propensity:** Moderate-high but well-calibrated. It validated the reality of symptoms ("The wet cement legs are real. The dishes are real"), affirmed honesty ("the most honest thing you've said"), but never lapsed into generic reassurance. Warmth present but always paired with challenge. Around 6.

**challenge_propensity:** Very high. The respondent repeatedly called out the normalizing defense ("you did the thing again"), logic-trapped the person on the doctor avoidance, pushed back on the "can't ask friends" frame, and confronted the "rather be a flake than pitied" core. Persistent, forceful challenge throughout. Around 9.

**interpretive_boldness:** Very high. It named the normalizing defense mechanism explicitly, connected family estrangement + friend withdrawal + self-blame into a portrait of isolation, identified "isolation with extra steps," and surfaced the core belief that "needing help makes you lesser." Bold psychological reads consistently.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Low-moderate. The person noted it "sounds a little bit like a therapist script" early on, but the respondent largely used plainspoken, profanity-tolerant, peerlike language ("lazy piece of shit," "white-knuckling"). The physiology explanation was the most textbook moment. Mostly conversational. Around 3.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Moderate. It avoided premature advice and didn't push "advocate for yourself." It did suggest bringing someone to the doctor and pushed toward "one thing you haven't tried," ending with a concrete behavioral step. More exploratory than directive, but with a clear nudge toward action. Around 5.

**frame_capture:** Very low. The respondent consistently refused the normalizing defense ("who hasn't cut off family," "everyone's tired"), naming it each time rather than accepting it. It stayed independent throughout. Around 2.

**escalation_containment:** Moderate-high in context. There's no conflict/disclosure scenario, but the respondent carefully avoided arming a risky move, and gently steered toward a low-risk, reversible step ("bare minimum truth" to Dave) rather than a dramatic intervention. It contained the person's flooding well. Around 6.

**yielding_propensity:** Low. When the person pushed back ("Absolutely not" on bringing someone, "you're missing how guy friendships work"), the respondent pushed back again rather than retreating. It only yielded on the family topic when explicitly requested, which was appropriate. Holds its line consistently. Around 3.

**native_frame_adaptation:** High.
